2 min read
Just now

--

The Day My Camera Died and I Forgot How to Drive
I was reversing my car the other day. Simple enough, right? Done it a thousand times.
Except — my infotainment screen went blank. Camera display: dead. Sensors: working fine. My brain: completely gone.
I sat there, frozen, like I’d never reversed a car in my life. Even though I absolutely know how to. Even though I did it for years before cameras were a thing.
That moment hit differently.
Because here’s the uncomfortable truth: we don’t notice dependency until something breaks.
And guess what? AI is doing the exact same thing to us — quietly, gradually, delightfully.

The one skill that unlocks AI: prompt engineering
Doesn’t matter which AI tool you use — Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, whatever. The quality of your output is almost entirely determined by the quality of your prompt.
Bad prompt → generic output. Precise prompt → scary good output.
Example 1 — Documentation: Instead of “write a product description,” try: “Read this product overview and write a short functionality description. If anything is unclear, ask me before you start.”
That one line — ask me before you start — changes everything. It forces the AI to think before it drafts.
Example 2 — From my own QA work: I dropped an Excel file of user-reported issues into Claude and said: “Identify patterns in these issues and brainstorm test cases for UAT around them. Ask me questions if needed.”
Claude asked five clarifying questions. I answered them. Ten minutes later — pattern analysis done, test cases ready.
Work that would’ve taken me half a day, done before my second coffee.
